# Risk Management Procedure

Owner: Mikhail Kurzin
Area: AI, InfoSec
Last edited time: January 19, 2026
Approvers: Dmitry Kushnikov
Document ID: PR-IS-01
Document Type: Procedure
Document application: All Contractors, All Employees
Effective date: March 1, 2022
Reviewers: Thu Nguyen, Mikhail Kurzin
Status: Published
Tag: ISO27001, ISO42001
Version: 3

<aside>

**Key points:**

- Assets vulnerabilities and threats
- Risk assessment
- Risk treatment
</aside>

<aside>

*This document describes the risk management process within Manychat.*

*The aim of risk management is to minimize adverse effects to business through identification, assessment, and prioritization of risks followed by actions to minimize, monitor, and control the risks.*

*A risk is a probability that a special event will occur in the future, linked with the negative impact this could have. Risk becomes a context (and is no longer a risk) when the risk event has occurred.*

</aside>

---

# Table of Contents

# 1. General

## **1.1 Purpose**

This document defines statements of the overall intentions and direction of actions related to the information security risk management at Manychat (hereinafter referred to as the Company), as well as a sequence of traceable actions, criteria, and roles related to the information security risk management process.

This document confirms the declaration and represents the Company’s Leadership team expectations that the information security risk management process, being the core process and integrated part of the Company’s Information Security Management System (ISMS), complies with the requirements of ISO/IEC 27001:2022 and adheres the principles of ISO 31000:2009.

For AI-enabled systems, this procedure also addresses AI-specific risks and impacts, including technical, ethical, legal, societal, and third-party risks, in alignment with ISO/IEC 42001, ISO/IEC 23894 (AI risk management), and ISO/IEC 38507 (AI governance).

### **1.2 Scope**

This procedure applies to all departments, divisions, sections, and services of Manychat and to all personnel of Manychat. It also applies to external organizations and their personnel who have been granted access to the infrastructure and services.

The document is reviewed at least annually, to ensure it meets the requirements of Manychat in-house, partner, customer, and external organizations, and Manychat business objectives.

For AI-enabled systems, this procedure applies to risks and impacts arising across the AI system lifecycle, including design, integration, deployment, operation, monitoring, and change management, as defined in the [AIMS Context and Scope.](https://www.notion.so/AIMS-Context-and-Scope-2e8b12e9aa1a80599c08c0add6f2be34?pvs=21)

# 2. Procedure

## **2.1 Summary**

The risk management process consists of the frequent assessment of risks (identification, analysis, estimation & evaluation of risks) followed by actions to minimize, monitor, and control the risks.

Information security risk management process is performed **at least annually** or **in case of significant changes, such as**:

- Organizational changes
- Technical changes
- Operational changes
- Legislation changes
- New threats & vulnerabilities
- Business & market changes

Information security risk management process is tailored to the Company’s context.

By establishing its context the Company:

- Defines its purpose
- Defines the ISMS scope
- Determines the external and internal issues that are relevant to its purpose and that affect its ability to achieve the intended outcomes of the ISMS
- Defines interested parties that are relevant to the ISMS and their requirements to information security
- Sets up its information security risk criteria
- Revises all of the above with regard to any changes taking place since the last information security risk management cycle

When planning for the ISMS, the Company considers its context and determine risks and opportunities that need to be addressed to:

- Ensure the ISMS can achieve its intended outcomes
- Prevent, or reduce, undesired effects
- Achieve continual improvement

The Company is planning actions to address these risks and opportunities, and how to:

- Integrate and implement the actions into its ISMS processes
- Evaluate the effectiveness of these actions

As part of its context establishment, the Company determines/revises information security risk criteria that include:

- The risk acceptance criteria
- Criteria for performing information security risk assessments (are identical to those of the whole risk management process).

The Company maintains documented information about its context ([ISMS Context and Scope](https://www.notion.so/ISMS-Context-and-Scope-2cbb12e9aa1a80c494d8fcee7ce95885?pvs=21)).

Risk assessment is focused on bringing together the relevant stakeholders to collaborate, identify, quantify and communicate all risks that might occur within the scope of the process for which risk management is executed.

Risk treatment is an action or a set of actions with the purpose to reduce the probability of a risk occurring and/or reducing the impact of a risk when it occurs.

The risk management process consists of the following steps:

- Establishing the context, includes assets and related threats
- Identifying the risks
- Classifying risks
- Choosing the treatment strategy
- Defining the necessary actions, for mitigating strategy - to reduce the impact or the probability of a risk
- Execute actions
- Evaluation of the residual risks

In case the mitigating actions did not result in an acceptable level of risk, new actions have to be defined to bring the level of risk further down to an acceptable level.

### 2.1.1 AI-Enabled Systems: Risk Management Scope and Triggers

In the context of AI-enabled systems, risk management extends beyond traditional information security risks and includes AI-specific risks and impacts arising from the design, integration, configuration, deployment, operation, and evolution of AI systems.

AI-related risks may be probabilistic, context-dependent, and emergent in nature, and may result in technical, ethical, legal, societal, or reputational impacts, including impacts on individuals and groups, even where confidentiality, integrity, and availability are not directly affected.

For AI-enabled systems, risk management activities include both AI risk assessment and AI system impact assessment, which are performed in an integrated manner and proportionate to the nature, scope, and potential impact of the AI system.

AI risk and impact assessments are initiated and reviewed, at a minimum, in the following circumstances:

- introduction of new AI-enabled features, workflows, or automation capabilities;
- material changes to AI behaviour, configuration, prompts, orchestration logic, or safeguards;
- onboarding, replacement, or significant change of third-party AI service providers;
- identification of AI-related incidents, adverse impacts, misuse, or unexpected behaviour;
- changes in legal, regulatory, contractual, or ethical requirements applicable to AI systems;
- periodic review of AI systems based on their risk level and operational criticality.

AI-related risks and impacts are assessed throughout the AI system lifecycle and are not limited to a single point in time. Risk assessments are reviewed when AI systems evolve, are repurposed, or are used in new contexts, in order to ensure continued alignment with intended use and responsible AI objectives.

## **2.2 Process description**

General scheme of a risk management process represented in Figure 1 applies to the Company’s information security risk management process.

![Figure 1. Risk management process](Risk%20Management%20Procedure/0cb5f1db-7362-40e2-894d-d914425134c9.png)

Figure 1. Risk management process

[](data:image/png;base64,)

The Company retains documented information of the information security risk management process as a **Risk Management Report (Template).**

### 2.2.1 Identify assets, vulnerabilities, and threats

The first step in risk assessment is the identification of all assets in the ISMS scope – i.e. of all assets which may affect confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information in the organization. Assets may include documents in paper or electronic form, facilities, applications and databases, key people, IT equipment, infrastructure, and external services/outsourced processes. When identifying assets and their value in accordance with [**Data Classification Policy**](https://www.notion.so/Data-Classification-Policy-2bfb12e9aa1a809aa48bd02294c10582?pvs=21) **,** it is also necessary to identify their owners – the person or organizational unit responsible for each asset.

For AI-enabled systems, assets may also include AI models provided or integrated via third-party services, prompts, orchestration logic, datasets under the Company’s control, AI configurations, outputs, logs, and dependencies on third-party AI services.

The next step is to identify all threats and vulnerabilities associated with each asset. Threats and vulnerabilities are identified using the catalogs included in the List of Threats TAB (Description of Threat Event and Threat Sources is taken from the best practices reference guides, such as NIST and ISO 27005 (can be exеtended and round out by other sources)). Every asset may be associated with several threats, and every threat may be associated with several vulnerabilities. Сonsistently assesses the applicability of threats to identified assets (focus on Highly Critical and Critical Asset Level for Business).

### 2.2.2 Identify risks

Considering its context, the Company identifies risks associated with the loss of confidentiality, integrity, and availability for information within the ISMS scope (ISMS critical assets) and the owners of these risks.

In the context of AI-enabled systems, risk identification shall consider not only confidentiality, integrity, and availability, but also:

- accuracy and reliability of AI outputs;
- bias, fairness, and non-discrimination risks;
- impacts on individuals, groups, and, where relevant, society;
- misuse, manipulation, or unintended use of AI functionality;
- dependency and concentration risks related to third-party AI providers.

An important goal of risk management is to identify and communicate the risks. Risks should be reviewed with all relevant stakeholders in order to get an agreement on the completeness and correctness of the identified risks.

The objective of risk identification is the early and continuous identification of events that, if they occur, will have negative impacts on either any Manychat process's ability to achieve performance or capability outcome goals or Manychat reputation. They may come from within the project, infrastructure, or from external sources. It can be any kind of threat, vulnerability, insecure configuration, weakness in an existing process, as well as non-compliance with any state regulation, which may cause financial loss and/or reputation damage.

During the identification of risk, the type of the risk shall be determined

| **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- |
| **IT** | Risk in the area of IT responsibility |
| **Cyber** | Risk in the area of CyberSec responsibility |
| **HR** | Risk in the area of HR responsibility |
| **Compliance** | Risk in the area of Compliance |
| **Ops** | Risk in the area of Business Operations, includes ISMS |
| AI | Risk arising from the design, integration, configuration, behaviour, or impact of AI systems, including technical, ethical, societal, and third-party AI risks |

### 2.2.3 Classify risks

Risk classification is performed to support prioritisation and treatment of identified risks. In the context of AI-enabled systems, classification also supports the identification of potential impacts beyond traditional information security concerns.

For AI-related risks, impact classification is informed by the results of the AI System Impact Assessment (AISIA) Procedure and includes impacts on internal and external parties.

Risk classes are used to prioritize (and communicate) the risks and associated actions. The risks will be classified into three classes: Unacceptable, Undesirable, and Acceptable. In the **Risk Management Report,** these risk classes will be colorized with respectively the colors: Red, Orange, and Green.

| **Risk Rating** | **Risk Class Color** | **Value** | **Mitigation** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Risk Rating >= 10 | Red | Unacceptable | Mitigation necessary |
| 5 <= Risk Rating < 10 | Orange | Undesirable | Mitigation optional |
| Risk Rating < 5 | Green | Acceptable | Mitigation not necessary |

In order to find the risk class, first each risk must be evaluated with respect to the probability of occurrence and impact of consequences.

The probability of occurrence has to be assessed in five levels and given a number corresponding with each of these five levels:

| **Risk Probability options:** | **Value** | **Description of the Probability options:** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Certain** | 5 | Considerable possibility of risk occurrence in 1y period. Risk will frequently occur during asset, project, process or business |
| **Likely** | 4 | Risks that have chances of occurrence in 3y period |
| **Moderate** | 3 | Legitimate possibility of risk occurrence in 5y period. Risk will probably occur during asset, project, process or business lifecycle. |
| **Unlikely** | 2 | Risks that have a low probability of occurrence in 7y period |
| **Rare** | 1 | Small possibility of risk occurrence in 10y period and beyond. Risk may occur during asset, project, process or business lifecycle |

For AI-related risks, probability reflects the likelihood that the AI system will behave in an unintended, harmful, or misleading manner within its intended or reasonably foreseeable use. Probability may be difficult to estimate due to the probabilistic and context-dependent nature of AI systems and shall therefore be assessed using best available evidence and expert judgement.

The impact has to be assessed also in five levels and given a number corresponding with each of these five levels:

| **Risk Impact options:** | **Value** | **Description of the Impact options:** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Extreme** | 5 | Risk has a significant impact (>=30% revenue loss). Large disruption of processes and key activities may occur for an undetermined amount of time. Large reputational damage may occur. |
| **Major** | 4 | (>=20% and <30% revenue loss) Risks with significantly large consequences |
| **Moderate** | 3 | Risk has a moderate impact (>=5% and <20% revenue loss). Disruption of processes and reputational |
| **Minor** | 2 | (>=1% and <5% revenue loss) If a risk will result in some damage, but the extent of damage is not too significant and is not likely to make much of a difference to the overall workload |
| **Trivial** | 1 | Risk has a negligible impact (<1% revenue loss). Business as usual/no reputational damages |

For AI-related risks, impact assessment shall consider not only adverse effects on Manychat as an organization, but also potential impacts on external parties, including individuals, groups of individuals, customers, end users, and, where relevant, society.
Impact ratings shall reflect the most severe credible harm, regardless of whether the harm is primarily financial, operational, legal, ethical, or societal in nature. Where impacts to external parties do not directly translate into immediate financial loss for Manychat, the impact level shall be determined based on the severity, scale, and reversibility of harm, including effects on rights, trust, fairness, or well-being.When assessing AI-related risks, the impact rating shall reflect the most severe credible harm, including non-financial impacts such as harm to individuals, unfair outcomes, misuse, or loss of trust, even if the likelihood of occurrence is low.

The risk rating of the risk is defined as probability multiplied by impact. *For example, a risk with a very low probability and a very low impact has a rating of 1 (1*1). A risk with a very high probability and a very high impact has a rating of 25 (5*5).*

The risk class can be determined via a table lookup. The lookup table is created by putting the probability on one axis and the impact on the other axis. Every combination of values for probability and impact is then assigned to one of the three risk classes and the corresponding table cell will be color-coded accordingly. In the table, the amount of issues with this probability and impact is shown. With such a table it is possible to quickly find the risk class given the impact and probability of a risk. See an example of a classification scheme in [Table-1].

![Screenshot 2025-12-03 at 2.51.42 PM.png](Risk%20Management%20Procedure/0faf5664-e76f-436a-afc1-48fa150e64c9.png)

[](data:image/png;base64,)

For AI-related risks, risks with low probability but high potential impact shall not be automatically deprioritised solely on the basis of the resulting risk class. Such risks may require additional mitigation, monitoring, or impact assessment in accordance with the AIMS risk and impact management process.

**Define mitigation actions**

For each risk, it must be decided which strategy should be applied.

| **N** | **Treatment strategy** | **Description** | **Explanation** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1. | **Accept the Risk** | Accepting the risk means that though the risk was identified and logged in Risk Management Tracker, no action will be taken. The Risk Owner accepts that it might happen and decides to deal with it if it does. The risk Status is being set to Accepted, and this risk becomes an Exception. Each Exception must be temporary and have a due date, after which it must be reviewed | Risk acceptance is the preferred strategy when the risk class is “Acceptable” (Green). No urgent action is needed. The risk will be accepted but it will be monitored for potential escalation to a more severe risk class. This strategy is a common option when the cost of other risk management options such as avoidance or limitation outweighs the cost of the risk itself. |
| 2. | **Avoid the Risk** | It is possible to make major changes in plans to avoid the risk. This is a good strategy for when a risk has a potentially large impact on planned activity | Risk avoidance is the opposite of risk acceptance. It is the action that avoids any exposure to the risk whatsoever. Risk avoidance is usually the most expensive of all risk mitigation options |
| 3.  | **Transfer the Risk** | The impact and management of the risk to someone else, this could be any other unit within ManyChat, or a third party. Another option of transferring the risk is its insurance. | Risk transfer is to outsource or insure the risk at a third party |
| 4. | **Mitigate the Risk** | Risk Owner provides ways to limit the impact of risk so that if it does occur, the problem it creates is smaller and easier to fix. And/Or controls for probability minimization | Risk reduction is the most common risk management strategy. This strategy reduces the risk’s probability and/or impact |
| 5. | **Get an opportunity (combination of circumstances expected to be favorable to objectives)** | This way of treatment may be used if something that would have a benefit to ManyChat if it happened | Taking or increasing the risk in order to pursue an opportunity |

If mitigation strategy chooses, mitigation actions must be defined, and if so which actions based on current and necessary for implementing IS controls.

The Company defines and applies the information security risk treatment process to:

- Select appropriate information security risk treatment options, taking into account the risk assessment results;
- Determine all controls that are necessary to implement the information security risk treatment options chosen;
- Compare determined controls with those in Annex A of ISO/IEC 27001:2022 and verify that no necessary controls have been omitted;
- Produce/revise the Statement of Applicability that contains the necessary controls and justification for inclusions, whether they are implemented or not, and the justification for exclusions of controls from Annex A of ISO/IEC 27001:2022;
- Formulate the information security risk treatment plan; and
- Obtain risk owners’ approval of the information security risk treatment plan and acceptance of the residual level of information security risks.

Information security controls are chosen from Annex A of ISO/IEC 27001:2022 or any other applicable source.

Where a risk is classified with the risk type “AI”, the outcome of risk classification is used to determine whether a dedicated AI system impact assessment is required and to support the selection of applicable controls from ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Annex A.

Each mitigation action must be evaluated with respect to customer expectations (if applicable), cost-to-benefit ratio, timing, technical feasibility, quality, and other parameters. Based on this evaluation a selection must be made of actions to be implemented. The decisions and mitigation actions must be recorded in the [**Risk Management Report (Template)**](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/u/0/d/1SnSlQoMa51qYBsx4HUeubBJ9cAfocs9n/edit).

For each risk, a Risk Action Owner is allocated.

### 2.2.4 Execute mitigation actions

The Risk Action Owner is responsible for the agreed action, to assure that the action is executed and to monitor and report the status and due date.

For AI-related risks, mitigation actions may include non-technical measures (e.g. usage constraints, transparency measures, or human oversight arrangements) in addition to technical controls.

### 2.2.5 Verification and Validation as Risk Treatment

For AI-enabled systems, risk treatment actions may include verification and validation (V&V) measures designed to confirm that the AI system meets documented requirements, intended use, and responsible AI objectives.

Verification and validation activities are selected based on the nature, severity, and potential impact of identified AI-related risks and may include, where applicable:

- verification of alignment between implemented AI functionality and documented requirements and specifications;
- validation of AI system behavior under expected and reasonably foreseeable conditions of use;
- testing of safeguards, constraints, and human oversight mechanisms;
- evaluation of AI outputs for accuracy, reliability, bias, or unintended behavior;
- confirmation that changes do not alter the intended use or introduce foreseeable misuse.

The need for completion or update of verification and validation activities shall be determined during risk treatment planning and documented in the Risk and Impact Assessment Report.

Verification and validation results shall be reviewed as part of residual risk evaluation to confirm that risks have been reduced to an acceptable level prior to deployment or continued operation.

### 2.2.6 Evaluate residual risks

After the risk is treated, the residual risk must be analyzed the same way the initial risk was. All steps must be captured in the risk record in [**Risk Management Report (Template)**](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1hLlsnrCg0aPyjmD1d_tcpFYKV-nPE4JR/edit?usp=drive_link&ouid=113646147365022607332&rtpof=true&sd=true), and if necessary risk actions status must be reviewed and updated. The risk Status must be set to Closed if the residual risk class is acceptable.

### 2.2.7 Archive results

After each risk management session, the resulting material (in specific the **Risk Management Report (Template)**) must be archived and can be used in future risk management sessions.

Where risks are classified as AI-related, archived records include references to supporting evidence relevant to AI risk treatment and evaluation.

### 2.2.8 Risk Management monitoring

To increase the productivity of the entire process and to avoid new risks, the Risk Management process must be regularly managed and monitored.

This must be performed in two ways:

- Service/System/Process Owners must review the **Risk Management Report (Template)** on an annual basis. Each Service/System/Process Owner must review all risk records assigned to them, including ones with Status set to Closed or Accepted. All Exceptions must be reviewed before they are overdue.
- The Internal Auditor reviews a random sample of risk records in the **Risk Management Report (Template)** [](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1orWHycHXV0na-g767Gz9LZuco8EWJFUF/edit#gid=47940169)during planned audit exercises.

Information security risk monitoring and review are performed as part of the risk management process and involve regular checking and surveillance.

The Company monitors information security risks on a continuous basis and reviews them over time to:

- Verify that chosen countermeasures and controls are implemented as planned;
- Obtain further information to improve the risk management process;
- Analyze and learn lessons from events (including near-misses), changes, trends, successes, and failures;
- Identify risk-impacting changes in the Company’s context;
- Identify emerging risks;
- Signals indicating changes in external dependencies relevant to identified risks (e.g. third-party services supporting AI-enabled features).

### 2.2.9 Risk Management Report structure

| **Field** | **Description** |
| --- | --- |
| **ID** | Incremented unique Risk ID which is assigned to each risk |
| **Risk description** | Detailed risk description, which must include the following:
1. Description of vulnerability/threat identified
2. Potential impact and harm caused by the event, including impacts on internal and external parties, as applicable.
3. Way of exploiting the threat (if applicable) |
| **Type** | Risk in the area of IT responsibility
Risk in the area of CyberSec responsibility
Risk in the area of HR responsibility
Risk in the area of Compliance
Risk in the area of Operations, includes Management System
Risk in the area of AI systems and AI-enabled functionality |
| **Assets affected** | Manychat asset name, link to Asset register (Assets Inventory List) |
| **Impact** | Extreme
Major
Moderate
Minor
Trivial
See Process description (above) |
| **Probability** | Certain
Likely
Moderate
Unlikely
Rare
See Process description (above) |
| **Risk Rating (SORT COLUMN)** | Сalculated value, See Process description (above)
For the "Overview top 5 Risks" the risks on the tab "Risk List" must have been sorted on "Risk Rating" (SORT COLUMN) manually. |
| **Current Controls (Annex A)** | Which IS or AI controls are already implemented (a reference to applicable ISO/IEC 27001 and/or ISO/IEC 42001 Controls list) |
| **Risk Class** | Unacceptable
Undesirable
Acceptable
Сalculated value, See Process description (above) |
| **Treatment strategy** | Accept the Risk
Avoid the Risk
Transfer the Risk
Mitigate the Risk
Exploit the Risk (opportunity)
See Process description (above) |
| **Action(s)** | Actions planned for risk mitigation |
| **Mitigation Controls** | Which IS/AI controls should be implemented (a reference to applicable ISO/IEC 27001 and/or ISO/IEC 42001 Controls list) |
| **Mitigated Impact** | Impact after mitigation |
| **Mitigated Probability** | Probability after mitigation |
| **Residual Risk Rating (SORT COLUMN)** | Risk after mitigation, calculated value
For the "Overview top 5 Risks" the risks on the tab "Risk List" must have been sorted on "Residual Risk Rating" (SORT COLUMN) manually. |
| **Residual Risk Class** | Risk after mitigation
Unacceptable
Undesirable
Acceptable
Сalculated value |
| **Risk (Actions) Owner** | Name of the individual who is responsible for risk treatment |
| **Target Date** | When the risk must be mitigated |
| **Action Status** | The status may be set to:
Open – The risk was reported and/or evaluated analyzed
In Progress – Risk Treatment plan is being developed
Accepted – The risk was accepted by the Business Owner without treatment
Closed – The residual risk was accepted after treatment |
| **Re-assessment Period** | Date on which the risk was last reviewed, updated and/or approved by management |
| **Comments** | Additional information |

# 3. Roles and Responsibilities

The CEO has the overall responsibility for Information Security at the Company, including information security regarding personnel and IT security.

Process specific roles and responsibilities:

| **Role** | **Role description** |
| --- | --- |
| **Leadership team** | • Provide clear inputs to Head of Information Security on what Leadership team is expecting from the ISMS;
• Approve the information security risk criteria;
• Take part in information security risk management and provide clear direction from the business perspective with this regard;
• Ensure that resources needed for the ISMS are available;
• Act as a Risk Owner for business risks;
• Inform Head of Information Security about significant changes known or foreseen;
• Cooperate and collaborate with others stakeholders upon information security risk management. |
| **Head of Security** | • Develop, implement, operate, take part in and improve an organization-wide information security risk management process;
• Initiate reviews of the Company’s context and take subsequent actions required;
• Liaise with the Company’s Leadership team with regard to information security risk management;
• Maintain the IS Risk Register up-to-date;
• Maintain the **ISMS Context and Scope** up-to-date;
• Maintain the Statement of Applicability up-to-date;
• Facilitate **Risk Management Report** filling during information security risk management process;
• Control that risks are treated as planned;
• Perform information security risk monitoring and review;
• Propose adequate countermeasures and controls to mitigate risks that cannot be accepted;
• Cooperate and collaborate with others stakeholders upon information security risk management. |
| GRC Lead | Coordinates identification, assessment, and treatment of AI-related risks and impacts in collaboration with Product, Engineering, Legal, and Compliance, in accordance with the AIMS. |
| **Process Owner** (Person with the accountability and authority to manage a Company’s process) | • Take part in information security risk management and provide expertise and support required with this regard;
• Provide inputs to the IS Risk Register;
• Act as a Risk Owner for risks related to their processes;
• Provide information regarding their processes and associated risks as required for the information security risk management process;
• Cooperate and collaborate with others stakeholders upon information security risk management. |
| **System/Service Owner** (Person, who is responsible for the overall procurement, development, integration, modification, operation, maintenance, and retirement of a Company’s information system/service) | • Take part in information security risk management and provide expertise and support with this regard;
• Provide inputs to IS Risk Register;
• Act as a Risk Owner for risks related to their systems;
• Provide information regarding their systems and associated risks as required for the information security risk management process;
• Cooperate and collaborate with others stakeholders upon information security risk management. |
| **Risk Owner** (Action owner) | • Review and approve **Risk Management Report** with regard to the appropriate risk treatment plan and acceptance of the residual risks;
• Provide resources for the approved risk treatment plans;
• Ensure that risks are treated as planned;
• Provide information on the risk treatment status to Head of Information Security upon a demand;
• Cooperate and collaborate with others stakeholders upon information security risk management.
• For risks classified with the risk type “AI”, the Risk Owner ensures that AI-specific impacts and dependencies are considered and documented. |

# 4. Compliance

The quality and effectiveness of Manychat’s management systems are demonstrated through the establishment, implementation, maintenance, and continual improvement of integrated management systems aligned with recognized international standards.

Manychat maintains an Information Security Management System (ISMS) aligned with ISO/IEC 27001:2022 and an Artificial Intelligence Management System (AIMS) aligned with ISO/IEC 42001:2023. These management systems provide a structured framework for managing information security risks as well as risks and impacts arising from the use of AI-enabled systems.

Risk management activities performed under this procedure support compliance with applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations across jurisdictions in which Manychat operates, including but not limited to:

- The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) (Regulation (EU) 2016/679);
- Personal data protection regulation in the countries or regions of operation (eg. CCPA);
- Canada’s Anti-Spam Law (or “CASL”);
- The CAN-SPAM Act;
- AI-related regulatory and governance requirements applicable to the use and operation of AI systems;
- other mandatory state regulatory acts including acts on commercial secrets and intellectual property.

This procedure supports a risk-based approach to compliance by ensuring that information security risks and AI-related risks and impacts are identified, assessed, treated, monitored, and documented in a consistent and auditable manner.

## **4.1 Enforcement**

Implementation and enforcement of this policy is ultimately the responsibility of all employees at Manychat. Security team may conduct ad-hoc checks to ensure compliance with this document without notice. Any violation shall require immediate corrective action. Violations shall be noted in the Manychat applications and responsible employees shall be dispatched to remediate the issue.

For Manychat employees, breaches of this policy may result in disciplinary action being taken and will be dealt with under the disciplinary process in accordance with state laws and regulations of the residence country. Any serious breaches or repeated breaches of this policy may be deemed by Manychat as gross misconduct and may result in disciplinary sanctions, including a remark, reprimand, and dismissal from work.

## **4.2 Exceptions**

Exceptions to this standard must be processed in accordance with the deviation handling rules by the Security team. All exceptions must be maintained taking into account associated risks, exceptions, and recorded approvals. Continuously detected deviations may trigger the changes or improvements of the measures defined in this document.
